original_abstract:
In this comprehensive review, we examine the burgeoning field of multilayer networks, a theoretical framework designed to capture the complexity of real-world systems where entities interact through multiple types of relationships, across different temporal scales, or via interdependent subsystems. While traditional network theory typically represents systems as simple graphs, most natural and engineered structures—from social communities and transportation infrastructures to biological pathways—are better described by a "multilayer" architecture.

We begin by establishing a general mathematical formulation that unifies the diverse and often conflicting terminology found in the literature. By introducing a precise framework based on "aspects" and "elementary layers," we demonstrate how established concepts—such as multiplex networks, interconnected networks, networks of networks, and interdependent networks—can be mapped onto a single, cohesive structure. This unification is further supported by a detailed dictionary of terms and a discussion of mathematical representations using both supra-adjacency matrices and higher-order tensors.

The paper provides an extensive survey of empirical data sets, illustrating how multilayer perspectives provide deeper insights into social dynamics, airline traffic, and gene expression. We also review the state of the art in network diagnostics, examining how fundamental properties like degree, clustering coefficients, and centrality are generalized for multilayer systems. Furthermore, we explore mesoscale structures, specifically focusing on the challenges of detecting community structure when nodes can belong to different groups across various layers.

Finally, we analyze the impact of multilayer connectivity on dynamical processes. We highlight how features like inter-layer correlations and dependencies can fundamentally alter the behavior of percolation, epidemic spreading, and synchronization, often leading to phase transitions and "new physics" that are absent in single-layer networks. We conclude with a summary of open challenges and an outlook on the future of this rapidly evolving discipline.
humanized_abstract:
Multilayer networks—a rigorous review Multilayer networks provide a theoretical framework to study systems composed of entities interacting through multiple kinds of relationships, at different time scales, or through interconnected systems. The common assumption in network science is that these systems can be efficiently modelled as simple graphs. However, almost all natural and man-made systems—social, transportation, biological—are multilayer in nature.

In this chapter we introduce the underlying mathematical formulation that unifies (hopefully) most of the conflicting terminology used in the scientific literature about complex systems of interconnected networks, or about multiplex, inter-connected, networks-of-networks, inter-dependent networks, etc. Indeed we will formally define aspects and elementary layers and provide a concise dictionary mapping from the most common terms in use to our definitions, while illustrating the adopted formalism at length with either supra-adjacency matrices and higher-order tensors.

We review some empirical data sets illustrating the opportunity offered by multilayer descriptions of social networks, transport networks such as airline transportation networks, and biological networks such as gene expression networks. We also provide a review of network diagnostics in multilayer systems, reviewing how quantities such as degrees, clustering coefficients, and centrality coefficients are defined and generalized to the multilayer case. We also discuss possible methods to characterize and detect mesoscopic structures in complex multilayer systems, with particular reference to the difficulties arising when multiplex networks present nodes with multiple community roles in different layers.

We are now ready to study the impact of multilayer connectivity on dynamical processes. We will see how, for instance, correlations and links dependencies between layers can dramatically affect percolation, epidemic spreading, and synchronization phenomena inducing often non trivial phase transitions and “new physics” that are not present in the corresponding single layer network and we will give a perspective on the challenges and future developments of this exciting research field.
